content.assessment: Of all proposals, this one is most explicitly structured around the lens's core criteria: it proposes piloting before national rollout, includes a comparison-region design for monitoring, and its own mechanism description flags the need to pair with zoning controls (linking to T4) to avoid a known failure mode observed in Finland's later experience. This self-aware sequencing and failure-containment design is a distinguishing strength under this lens, though workforce capacity and pilot representativeness remain open gaps.
opportunities: Pilot design allows isolation of Hungary-specific effects from confounds that limited interpretability of Finnish and Polish precedents
Built-in linkage to zoning/choice controls (T4) creates a natural sequencing pathway toward a more failure-contained combined reform
strengths: Explicit pilot-before-national-decision design directly satisfies the lens's pilotability and monitoring criteria

Mechanism description proactively identifies its own failure mode (choice-driven re-segregation absent zoning controls) and proposes linking to a complementary lever (T4), showing unusually strong ex-ante adaptation planning
threats: Even a successful, well-designed pilot faces low institutional durability precedent (Poland's reversal), meaning strong pilot evidence may not translate into sustained national policy adoption

Centralized governance bottlenecks (F-live-implementation_planning-03) could still constrain how pilot findings are processed and acted upon at national level, limiting the practical value of the pilot's feedback loop
weaknesses: Same workforce capacity constraints identified for T2 apply to any comprehensive-schooling shift, even at pilot scale, since teacher deployment and training would need to shift within pilot regions (F-live-implementation_planning-07)
Volunteering pilot regions may not be representative, limiting the pilot's ability to generate generalizable implementation lessons for national sequencing decisions
